title: Lists
overview: Lists are Python's way of storing collections of items. They're flexible, powerful, and everywhere in Python programs.

blocks.title: What is a List?
blocks.body: A **list** is an ordered collection of items enclosed in square brackets []. You can store any type of data.
bullets: Lists keep their order (first item stays first)

blocks.title: A List is Like a Shopping List
blocks.text: You write items in order: first eggs, then milk, then bread. You can add items, remove items, or check what's at position 2. Python lists work exactly the same way.

blocks.title: Think Of A List As A Shopping List

leftPanel.label: Real Life Analogy
leftPanel.title: Shopping List
leftPanel.description: Items in order. Add, remove, or access by position.

centerPanel.label: Programming Concept
centerPanel.textContent:
A list is an ordered collection:
• Items stay in order
• Access by position (index)
• Add/remove items anytime

blocks.question: Does fruits[0] give you the first item or the last item in the list?
blocks.answer: fruits[0] gives you the first item ('apple'). In Python, indexing starts at 0, not 1. So index 0 = first item, index 1 = second, and so on. This is like saying 'position 0' on your shopping list is the very first item you wrote down.

blocks.title: Creating and Printing Lists
blocks.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print(fruits)

mixed = ["hello", 42, True, 3.14]
print(mixed)
print(type(fruits))

blocks.title: Create a Shopping List
blocks.instructions: Create a list with 3 items. Use a for loop to print each with a number.
blocks.hint: Use range(len(groceries)) to get indices. Add 1 to display starting from 1.

blocks.title: Accessing Items with Index
blocks.body: Each item in a list has a position called an **index**. Python uses zero-based indexing.
bullets: Index starts at 0: fruits[0] is the first item
Negative index counts from end: fruits[-1] is last
Slicing extracts a portion: fruits[1:3] gets index 1 and 2
len(list) gives the number of items

blocks.title: Indexing and Slicing
blocks.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "date", "elderberry"]
print(fruits[0])
print(fruits[-1])
print(fruits[1:4])
print(fruits[:3])
print(fruits[2:])
print(fruits[::2])

blocks.title: Access List Elements
blocks.instructions: Create a list of 5 numbers. Print the first, last, and middle elements.
blocks.hint: Use index 0 for first, -1 for last, and len(nums)//2 for middle.

blocks.title: Working with List Methods
blocks.body: Lists have built-in **methods** that let you modify and analyze them.
bullets: append(item) — adds item to the end
len(list) — returns the number of items

blocks.title: List Operations
blocks.code:
tasks = ["study", "exercise", "code"]
print(tasks)
tasks.append("read")
print(tasks)
tasks.remove("exercise")
print(tasks)
tasks[0] = "review"
print(tasks)
print(f"Total: {len(tasks)}")

blocks.title: Student Marks Manager
blocks.instructions: Start with [78, 92, 85, 63, 88]. Add 95, remove the lowest, sort, and print the average.
blocks.starterCode:
marks = [78, 92, 85, 63, 88]
marks.append()
marks.remove()
marks.sort()
print(f"Marks: {marks}")
print(f"Average: {sum(marks)//len(marks)}")
blocks.solution:
marks = [78, 92, 85, 63, 88]
marks.append(95)
marks.remove(min(marks))
marks.sort()
print(f"Marks: {marks}")
print(f"Average: {sum(marks)//len(marks)}")
blocks.hint: Use append() to add, min(marks) with remove() for lowest, sort() to sort.

keyTakeaways.text: Lists are ordered collections in square brackets []
Index starts at 0; negative indices count from end
append() adds items, remove() deletes items
sort() orders lists, len() counts items

whatYouLearned: How to create and print lists
How to access items with indices
How to use list methods: append, remove, sort
How to build a marks manager
